title: Sheyla Ruiz -Hiring Manager Screen | Community Lead
meeting_title: Sheyla Ruiz -Hiring Manager Screen | Community Lead

transcript.text: Hi, Jason.
Sheila.
I'm so sorry I'm late.
How are you doing?
All good.
I'm doing great.
Happy Friday.
Happy Friday.
There's so much sun where you're at.
Like, it's raining here in Oakland today, but you look like you're in almost, I don't even know.
The sunniest place possible.
Yeah, I'm in Florida, so that's accurate.
Oh, you're in Florida.
Okay, good.
I am.
I have Florida ceiling windows and the light.
It's just incredible.
So thank you.
I miss those days.
I used to live in Toronto, like in the city, and it's like condos with lots of light.
And now in the Bay Area, it's all like really old houses, you know, like outdated.
Yeah.
My partner's from Oakland, so actually, I'm missing a bit.
Yeah, yeah.
I'm actually, you're like, I miss the East, and I'm like, I miss the West, but the West Coast weather.
Yeah, yeah.
you.
It is so cool and fresh.
guess there's trade-offs regardless, but a little bit of fun.
I mean, all things considered, I'm very, very happy here.
I don't miss winters, you know?
Yeah.
I mean, though, you're in Florida, so maybe it's best of both worlds.
Oh, but yeah.
Yeah, but it's really great to meet you.
I know you talked to Tucker already, but I mean, this conversation really, you know, I mean it to be as open-ended as possible.
Like, I know you probably have plenty of questions, like what we're even doing.
And I'd like to maybe start there, just like what we're doing, what this community that we're building even is, and like the kind of help that we're looking for there for someone to drive.
And then, I mean, obviously, we'd love to learn more about you as well.
I looked at Monarch money, and we've been using this thing called lunch money, I think?
Yeah.
a while.
It's like super bare bones.
I wish it kind of had a mobile app.
I use like the mobile web thing, but it does feel like it's built for like a nerd a little bit.
So like it gives me all the toggles that I need.
So my wife like loves it.
Yeah, their UI is super cool.
Yeah.
As a tooling geek, I really love their aesthetic.
Also as a gamer, so I totally understand.
And yes, I am so curious about what you're building.
So please tell me everything.
I have so many questions.
Yeah, I mean, we'd love to start there.
But, you know, in general, I think you can think about the company is we're really trying to figure out just like what knowledge work is like.
And, you know, like in 2025 and beyond with AI for just like kind of regular non-technical people.
like marketers, people who work in sales, because we just see this like crazy gap between kind of engineering and everybody else.
You know, for the most part, like marketers.
marketers.
I think still work kind of through Google Docs and we're constantly kind of writing and rewriting like, you know, things that are really important.
I think things like Notion have made things slightly a little bit better, but there's still kind of just this huge gap in like how we do work.

And you can see how AI is just like amplifying that, you know, and I think if that continues, I think AI one day will just kind of like replace everybody, which I think nobody, you know, really, really wants that.
So our first kind of bet as a company is like, okay, let's just kind of figure out how to do this work ourselves first.
So we started GrowthX, the agency, you know, generally for the agency, we own some piece of like a company's growth function, usually around content.
So, you know, as an example, like Webflow, they want more developer awareness.
So they come to us and we work with them to figure out like, hey, what's the content and strategy you have to put out there to kind of shape that.
So if you go to Webflow's website, go to like Webflow slash integrations, like we're creating just this like huge web of documentation.
So developers, when they want to build on Webflow, they have like something to look at to help them.

And I guess what's different about our whole approach is like instead of hiring just like a big kind of pool of writers and then doing the whole, you know, writer management thing, we're building kind of systems and machines that create content instead.
And these systems aren't like totally just like, you know, hands off the wheel, like end to end.
It's like these systems are kind of a combination of like AI agents that do certain things really well, like research or writing or like, you know, tone.

And then human experts that do things really well, like, you know, for writing kind of web developer docs for Webflow, we kind of need a web developer in the mix, just seeing how things are, you know, and steering kind of this whole system.
And then would it.
It enables people to do is just like, I think we publish at the moment, like 40, like 30 to 40 articles a week.
You know, we have like almost 300 now published.
It's just like a totally different scale to doing this work.

And, you know, I think if you're, if you're trying to win the whole kind of awareness war, you know, especially with AI now, you just need to kind of put more content out there and make the quality higher and higher.
Yeah.
So that's what growthx is.

And then I'm sure you have a lot of questions, but, but, I guess like tying it to the community, like our whole kind of marketing as a company is, is really just about like teaching people how to do this stuff and like being kind of very transparent with like, we're probably one of the only companies in the world that's actually like just figuring this stuff out, you know, and, and we'll admit, like we have, we're not fully sure on how to do any of this yet, but like compared to all the other tools out there, some of them like offer platforms, but like none of them are actually like working with like the fastest growing company.
You know, like, Ramp and Webflow and Lovable and, like, just, like, in the trenches, like, figuring this stuff out.
So our whole marketing is just kind of, like, doing the work and then, like, sharing that with everybody.
And, like, you know, some people, they look at it and they're like, all right, I can do this myself.
But, like, a lot of people kind of see it and it's like, okay, this kind of looks a little bit more like engineering and product than it does, like, hiring a bunch of marketers to do content.
And then they come to us to kind of outsource that function.
So that's kind of how everything started.
And then what we, you know, like, what we quickly realized is, like, the content itself was just, like, so valuable.
Like, people were just kind of paying for it on their own.
So, like, I remember, like, our workshops are kind of, a pretty big anchor to, like, our community.
And, like, I think one day it was just, like, randomly, like, we made, like, there be, like, a ticket price for it.
And, like, there were, like, multiple people who paid, like, $500 just to, like, come to a workshop.
But...
Yeah.
Right.
And then, you know, that was like the beginning of it.
And then we kind of like packaged, you can kind of see it on AIledgrowth.com.
We like packaged a lot of the content into a community.
And then, you know, we were like now charging people to kind of consume that content or trying to like position the community is essentially, it's like a totally separate brand.
And it's like, you know, hey, as a marketer, there's just like so many things going on, right?
Like your work is changing because like AI just changes how you work, you know?
And like your target is also changing because now you're kind of marketing to humans and, but you're now also marketing to AIs and your channels are all completely different.
Like kind of don't go on that journey alone, right?
Like come kind of learn with people that are figuring this out every day.
And yeah, and be a part of that community.
And now I think we have 70 paying members.
We keep tweaking the pricing right now.
The pricing is...
It's like 150 bucks up front and then 30 bucks a month.
And it's kind of crazy because they're just kind of paying to like almost like consumer marketing in a way.
And like that's what we're trying to kind of find an owner for and continue to grow.
So I'll stop there.
Yeah, I'm not surprised that you have found interest and success because I think there is obviously an abundance of AI tooling out there.
And the industry is moving so quickly.
Everyone wants to adopt AI, but it is very isolating as well, right?
So this kind of solves that problem where it's like you don't have to, you know, yeah, you can look at the tool and work, learn your way through it.
And maybe, you know, pick up from thought leadership or other peers here and there, how you might accelerate or increase your skills.
But the reality is that there is no real guidance from the human perspective on connecting the dots.
And it can be really isolating, right?
And also,
So it's like, what are really the best practices?
That's why I always like peer-to-peer-led communities are so effective.

Even from a content perspective, if you're consuming content that was created by people who live your pain points and therefore are addressing them directly is hugely different than saying, hey, look at this great AI tool, use it.
Or like, we can use it for you.
So, yeah, I think there's a really big component with the way AI is trending right now that it can be really isolating.
And so I think this community is so important for that reason.

And I also think there's an incredible amount of information out there and so many different perspectives that it can become very overwhelming for leadership to figure out like, okay, well, what is really the right direction?
You know, like how, how will this, like when we, when we think about marketing, is that just a single point of touch, not a single touch point?
There's like the entire user funnel, right?
So executives typically are always thinking along those lines, like, yeah.
If do this, how will it affect the funnel down the line?
And so all of these things combined, it can just be so confusing, isolating, and feel very much like I will invest in this, but I don't know what it will really give me in the end.
And so building a community around proven, this is the content, this is why it moves the needle, this is the impact that it had, it's just really important, I think.
And that's why Tucker was telling me a little bit about this.
I'm like, we're a differentiator as well, right?
Like you have this amazing content generating engine, and of course, it's an amazing product, product marketing is super strong, in my opinion.

And that in itself is really enticing, but there is that part of it where it could become just another tool that becomes almost like, okay, I really want to use this tool, but now there's so much for me to choose and get involved in.
How do I know this is the right, you know, thought leadership or insight that I need?
And so I think the community component is what makes it super interesting, because I think it solves that gap.
So yeah.
Super curious about it all.
I think it's really interesting.

And I love that you're thinking about community now, instead of something down the road where maybe you have a larger cohort and you're like, oh, maybe we should put together something, you know, like naturally having a community at the center of this is a huge differentiator, in my opinion.
Yeah, I mean, I mean, I love that kind of breakdown.
And it's really good to hear from your side, as well, because I think one thing we haven't had the bandwidth to do is just like even talk to a lot of community members.
We kind of have two tiers to it.
Like we have the kind of executive tier where it's like a little bit less about like, you know, come look at a three hour workshop.
It's like more come to a private dinner and, you know, come hang out and talk and meet people who are going through kind of the same challenges.
But yeah, but I mean, I think like to me, the main differentiating thing is like, we're not
At least at the moment, like pushing a tool, like, you know, like our end goal isn't really that.
Our end goal is kind of just like to teach you stuff and make you feel like that knowledge is valuable and then get you to pay for that.
So I think it's like a lot more aligned than like most other communities where like, you're kind of just like forcing a tool.
And I think with AI, you undoubtedly get to this point where you're like being a little bit too kind of liberal, like pitching it as magic, you know?
And like, for the most part, like not everyone can do what, you know, the best kind of can do with like these AI tools because they don't know how to prop, they don't know how to use it.
It's true.
And we don't have that.
And then, and the other thing is like, even the people that build the tools, they've never actually done the work.
So like the things that we do and like talk genuinely about, like overlap a lot closer with like our audience and what the struggle they have to go through, right?
Like for the most part, like, yeah, like we're, we're accountable to clients for what our audience.
It's accountable to their kind of, you know, bosses and companies for.
So that I found kind of also that just being like fundamentally different.
So, yeah, but I mean, I think like unpacking that and figure out how to like pour gas on it is like a lot of kind of what we need to do, you know, just very small at the moment.
Yeah.

But, you know, like at this current stage, so I've built communities for a lot of different tech companies and a lot of different verticals, whether it's like agencies or B2B or like direct to consumer.
And one thing that I found almost always is that at this point, whether it's me building from zero to one or scaling existing, when you have a small cohort, it's honestly like the juiciest time.
Because it's the time where you can really identify, like, what is the, what about the value prop was so strong that it attracted this, this cohort specifically, it's so focused.
And then you can kind of scale from there.
But you're right.

Also, like, as you continue to deliver value through community or different areas of the product, right, outside of the actual practical tool, it will become inherently more important to understand the voice of the user.
So marketing as a discipline, obviously, is one thing, right?
That's a whole other thing.
But then the other thing is, like, the unique challenges that come with people who are making decisions for recently complex products that are evolving value props and AI.

So a lot of marketers now are facing this challenge where it's like, before it was like, you have to market this tool, this product, this service, you have to do it well, you have to extol the virtues and kind of like, you know, do the best that you can.
AI came in, it's like, now we're going to help you do that.
But now it's even further than that.
Now it's like, how does AI might become part of your value prop?
And you have to be able to vocalize that.
And how do you connect those dots so that it feels very much a natural part of what you're offering your consumers?
So I think you're at a really great point.
To invest in community further, because this is where you're going to get the most concentrated direction from, like the most concentrated signals from your cohort.
So small but mighty, in my opinion.
Is there, just because like, I mean, this is like totally new to me a little bit, like just at where we're at, right?
Like as a company, I mean, we're a year in, grown a lot, like as far as the agency goes, the community, I think we have like, you know, 70 paying members.

And then maybe like 100 plus, you know, other folks that have accounts there, our workshops get maybe, like they're kind of like, it's not totally like cohesive at the moment, but the workshops get, you know, from 500 to like 1000 registrants usually.
Like, how would you think about, like, what should I be prioritizing, I guess, at this point, you think?
Like, I think it's less about choosing one or the other and more about.
Thinking, what does the journey look like?
I'll explain.
So workshops, right?
Workshops are great, but is there a path to action after the workshop?
Are there practical steps that people who attend can actually incorporate into their work stream or whatever it is that they're trying to do?
I think that's even more important, right?
Keeping the momentum going.

It's really easy for community members or people who are consuming content to lose momentum, whether it's, you know, through, like, absorbing it through a workshop environment or through, like, a gated community.
It doesn't really matter.
It's, like, what do they do with it?
So it's more about, like, okay, what is the impact that's being had on these?
Let's say you have however many registrants and so many of them show up, like, 30, 40, 50%.
I don't know what your attendance rate is.
What's the follow-through, right?

What's the follow-through that's going to leave that lasting impression on their work where they can actively say, this worked for me and now I want to bring more people, more awareness into this specific engagement rather than the activity itself?
So I would look more closely at, like.
What is the after, right?
Like so often we focus on the performance of the things that we do in the moment, let's say like a webinar, right?

But what's the after, you know, like when you think about collaborating with brands like Lovable or like Ramp or Webflow, you talked a lot about like from a content perspective, Webflow specifically with developers, right?
It's the same thought, like, yes, we can create a page that the content focuses on what developers care about because that's the ICP we're trying to attract.

But unless you are giving practical actionable items for developers to actually follow this API docs, know, getting started Webflow, build this, build that, it feels stagnant almost in a world where people are surrounded by options on what's next.
You have to give them that follow through.
Yeah, that makes sense.
How would you, like, how would we do that?
So like the workshops, like, they're not really interactive.
I mean, they're inactive in the sense they can.
Ask questions and we answer them kind of like periodically through the workshop, but yeah, but in the workshop it does stop.
There's so many things you can do.
For example, you can create an interactive experience after the workshop, whether that is a checklist of things that were discussed in the workshop, like work through these things.
You can share within the community as you work through them from even like a matchmaking mentorship sort of program where it's like, these are the people who attended.
These are the people who have expertise in this.
You know, how do we pair people together so they can knowledge share?
And maybe that will lead to even more content.
There's so many things you can do post-workshop specifically.
And that applies to any part of community.
Like for me, like what has made the communities I feel and feel so successful is that it isn't, it's never in isolation.
Nothing is ever done in isolation.
It's always thought of what, what part of like, it's like breadcrumbs, like what is, what is the next breadcrumb?
And what is this leading to and how is this going to help people feel?
Like they're not just taking an action, but more they're on a journey.
Makes sense.
I think, I mean, most communities that I've seen like are attached to something and then that product kind of becomes like a little bit of the kind of engine to get more members.
But I think maybe like one difference for us is like, I mean, we only have like 60 customers like for growthx.
And it's like, it doesn't really contribute that many people.
I mean, there's some play to like get the teams of our customers like into the community.
But like, apart from that, we don't really have like an engine to kind of get more members, you know, to the community.
Like, yeah, I guess that's been something I haven't really figured out how to do.
Like, do you have any thoughts on how that would work?
in my opinion, every community, so what you're saying is true.
Especially around like tech companies, right?
Like their product becomes the value prop for the community.
In my opinion, in the way that I build communities, the community itself needs to have its own value prop independent of the product or the tool, right?
So let's say, for example, that in Monarch's case, right?
Like the tool is a personal finance, like network, personal budgeting platform.
That's the tool.
But the value prop for the community isn't about utilizing the tool to the maximum capacity.
It is about uniting people who have similar goals from a personal finance perspective and helping them feel like, A, other people also have similar struggles, challenges.
And B, there are actionable ways based on other people's experiences that they can learn from each other and they can get to whatever goals that they have.
And that's the value prop.
It's about everyone has financial goals.
Everyone has access to these tools, you know, whether it's Monarch or anything else.
But the most important value prop is, can I go to a place where, A, my challenges are seen?
I'm.
I'm in a crowd of people who are either going through very similar situations or have similar goals.
And can I walk away with the certainty that other people have taken X, Y, and Z steps and it has led them to success?
And I can also walk that path.
And then obviously there's like the validation part of it.
And then, you know, there's obviously like certain parts that tie back to the product.
Like how do I use the product as a tool as part of this process?
So it's not the value prop of the community and it's full.
It's just a very small part.
And I think that's like the winning formula, to be honest, when a community becomes too tied into the value prop, it becomes more of an echo chamber for marketing.
And that's not really building community.
So for Monarch, like what, what does the community look like?
So for Monarch, the community looks like people who are actively helping each other, whether it's validating, like, let's say, you know, I.
I am either in a place where I'm very comfortable financially and I just don't know what to do next.
And what would you do?
What have you done?
What has worked for you?
To people who are now saying, like, you know, especially now with the way that things are changing, how are you adapting from a human perspective?
Like, what does life really look like for you today?
What adjustments are you making?
So, yeah, the product itself will help you do these things on a practical level.
But on the community side, it's more about, like, okay, how would you approach this as if you're talking to a friend or someone who you really trust an advisor, right?
How do you, how do, how can I adjust my lifestyle to help me navigate this new situation?
And that is a completely different value prop.
Like, human connection, insight from people who have done it before, who are either, you know, like, professional, like, certified professional planners, whatever it is.
It's very different than the tool itself.
But does that, like, I guess, like, practically, like, what does that look like for monarchy?
Are these like little meetups in person?
Is there like a discussion forum?
So Monarch has a super active subreddit community.
Then they also have a lot of events that they do.
So whether it's more of like advisory events, like webinars, like how, you know, debt repayment, how do I approach that?
What's the best way for me to tackle that?
Or let's say like financial fitness.
What is the best way for me to tackle explain the year?
what is the path to success?
It's more about teaching actual financial literacy versus just discussing the product.

And then of course, like there are definitely product-led discussions as well, like tooling and making sure that not just advocating for the product, but making sure that the product is evolving with the needs of people.
Like, you know, I think it's really important and has been really influential in Monarch's success that we listen very carefully to what people are saying, not just about the product.
Or,
of like feature X, feature Y, sure.
But it's more about like, hey, your demographic is changing.
The needs and the pain points are changing.
How is the product adapting to that from a practical perspective?
Whether that means how are we reaching our audience, our new ICP?
How are we changing our value props?
How are we addressing from a UX perspective?
Like some mix of everything.
So like practically like the community is the subreddit for the most part.
Like does it extend into real life or like virtual, you know, events or anything like that at all?
Or is it mainly just kind of managing this like forum that's happening on Reddit?
So we do do workshops and webinars.
We haven't done in-person events yet, but mostly it's going to be like led to sessions fair.
We also have an invite only discourse that's for like super engaged community members for beta testing, just general like more power user feedback.
So it.
It comes across whether there's like live voice events over discourse, webinars, or just discussions in the actual subreddit, which is more forum style.
We do a lot of things like AMAs, a lot of different types of events to keep people engaged.
Understood.
So like, I guess for Monarchs community, like what's your role on that team?
Or is it like kind of like, are you managing that community like within marketing, I guess that rolls up too?
My role focuses on first setting the vision and direction of the community and making sure that it ties back into organizational goals.
And what I mean by that is a community can be a very amorphous concept.
And depending on like what the actual goals are, it can be very different.
So my job is to think about what the, define the value prop for community.
So in this case, it's financial literacy, wherever you are, we're going to meet you there and we're going to make it accessible for you.
We're going to give you the content that you need.
We're going to give you the peer support that you need and making sure that that's supported throughout.
Our entire marketing team.
So let's say we have a head of advice and planning, right?
Like how can we bring her in to do more thought leadership and more expert-led guidance?
We have an advisor program where we can bring people who specialize in different things, whether it's finances for couples or maybe it's retirement.
We bring those expert voices in and we do more work that makes the monarch experience extend far outside of the product.

So my job is to make sure that we're meeting our users at every level and making sure that I'm connecting the dots, not just from a forum, but throughout the funnel of as soon as they're on board, how can we deliver value outside of the product?
How can we engage with them?
Okay.
So then the kind of metrics that you think about the most are like, is it more about kind of like organic kind of user acquisition of new users?
Is it more about like retention, kind of like how many touch points you get people to get them to a certain spot?
I think that our community really affects both acquisition and retention.

It's a huge retention lever for obvious reasons, but it's also surprisingly a big acquisition lever because thanks to AI, a lot of platforms specifically like Reddit are being surfaced a lot more when people ask relevant questions around personal finance.
So we're seeing a lot of discoverability there, but also like as a differentiator from the product, like, yeah, you can use this tool, but also there's a community to support you.
So it becomes a funnel for people to come through and join the product.
We see a huge spike in retention for people who are part of the community versus people who are not.
And that really has a lot to do with the fact that people feel a closeness and alignment with the company's vision, with the direction through the community.
That's like another big part of the community, like brand building and awareness.
Even if it feels incidental, it's.
It's intentional and it's really effective.
Yeah.
Like I'm scrolling through right now.
It's like, it's kind of like mostly, I would say like about questions about the product, but I see things pop up like, like this one right here.
What is it?
It's like somebody asking for, yeah, many financial advisors suggest a guardrail approach to retirement spending.
Like that, that feels a lot more like kind of just like, Hey, I'm just, you know, coming here to get some advice on, on my finances.
I guess like what's happening behind the scenes for you to kind of shape, like what is happening here in this community?
I have a really close relationship with the community.
So I'm constantly speaking to them, listening to them.
There's also quite a bit of analytics.
So like we look at themes within conversations.
We look at product adoption specifically for feature sets.
So for example, if we're seeing a focus on people using investment for budgeting, then I'll think, okay, there's a content.
There's also a lot of concentration when we look at product questions.
There's usually a more nuanced question underneath, right?
So someone may be asking, like, how do I use this?
what they really want to know is like, hey, I really need to make sure that when tax season comes around, I'm really prepared, and that I'm in the best position possible.
How do I get that done?
Yeah, a lot of conversation, a lot of like, when you mentioned that you're at a stage that you haven't been able to connect with your community, I think that's so important.
And I would really encourage you to pursue that.
think this role specifically, would be a really good connector for that.
Like, ultimately, the role of someone like me, or like a community league is to be that connector.
And whether that's internally through teams connecting the value, or whether that's externally connecting members to either the value prop or to each other.
So whatever knowledge that they need to access to help them feel like they're getting a complete experience versus just like here, here's a tool, use it.
Best of luck.
Here's some support articles, you know.
Right.
Was the community for, like, any of the other companies you've run community at, like, very different than kind of, like, this form for Monarch?
Definitely.
The community I ran at Couchbase was completely developer-focused.
And so that was really, really different.
Very technical, very much more like the proof is in the pudding.
So we would do a lot of events, did a lot of, we did a series of global events with developers, just giving them, like, hands-on application, doing live coding sessions with them.
Just really, really different.
At Duda, for example, I worked with all their, the majority of their customer base are agencies.
So I dealt with a lot of people who were doing, like, marketing for the agency or founders and co-founders.
And so the community was completely different.
was a lot of thought leadership events, doing, like, events where it wasn't necessarily us speaking to them, but it was other members, other thought leaders within the space.
speaking to each other.
So the focus was totally different.
The deliverables were completely different.
What people walked away from from those sessions as leaders from their agencies was totally different than what a developer might walk away from.
And that's the beauty of community, right?
Like you can change it.
You can make it really amorphous depending on what audience you're addressing.
And it's important for you to know the difference and kind of target your audience appropriately.
That makes sense.
I know we're close to time, but like, did you have any questions that I can help answer?
wow.
I didn't even realize.
Sorry about that.
Yeah.
So, you know, you asked me an important question, which is what metrics do I pay attention to today at Monarch retention and acquisition?
And I'll kind of flip the question and ask you, right?
Like this community that you're thinking about, where do you see it fitting in from a metric perspective?
Jason to your goals today.
Obviously, this can evolve over time and will likely will, but I'm just curious to see how you're thinking about it today.
Yeah, I mean, I'll say like this kind of notion of keeping it just like totally aligned with like what people care about and just being valuable there.
Like, honestly, for as long as possible, I would like the metric to just be like, how many members do we have?
Like, if you have to boil it down to one, like how many paying members do we have?
And then it's like, you know, they're not going to pay and retain unless they're getting value.
I'd say by far, that's like just the metric for this community.
Like, I would love this community to be like, you know, like an Exit 5 or like the person that runs our events here used to run events for Pavilion.
She's, you know, it's like kind of a marketing leader community.
There's like things like Reforge or Maven.
And like, I just want the community to be almost its own product.

I think our approach to the company, as weird as it is, is to kind of like almost have like this portfolio approach to things where we don't want like individual things to just kind of serve as like the marketing channel for another kind of entity.
Right now, our agency is by far the biggest thing.
But like, I would love, you know, the community to be like significant as far as revenue goes.
Like we're launching products.
I would love, you know, for those, like for me to like grow those into being significant as well.
Yeah.
So, so that's probably number one.
And then, I mean, like almost like without any work at all, it's like already generating pipeline, you know, for the agency.
We're going to have products that would like generating kind of like user acquisition or new kind of customers for the product, I think will be a part of this as well at some point.
drop but...
When...
What...
What...
Literally right now, like all I care about is like, you know, our goal for the end of the year is like we have 70 members right now.
trying to see like, okay, is it possible to get like a thousand at the end of the year?
I'm trying to think about it like that, you know?
Yeah, yeah, that makes total sense.
And I love that you're thinking about it as its own product.
Honestly, that's the way that I would think about it as well.
It should be strong enough to stand on its own.
Like even apart from anything else, like I really want to be a part of this community.
And then obviously that will naturally lead into other areas of the business.
Another question that I have for you, Tucker mentioned something super interesting that down the road you may consider doing an open source community or just making a product more open source.
Is that something that you're thinking about?
Tell me more about that.
Yeah, I mean, we haven't really launched any products yet.
So we have like, so like the goal was always like, hey, we run this agency.
Right now the agency runs on our own platform.
it's like, you know, the more.
We learn about like how you should work, the more kind of we're going to build to kind of power that, right?
And, you know, our goal eventually is to kind of launch these into the world.
And I feel like nothing is like firm enough where I would present it as like, you know, hey, we're going to do this.

Like the open source thing Tucker's referring to, I'm not, I'm honestly not even sure what he's referring to, but like the, the, the base layer that's powering a lot of our like AI workflows and automation is like more of a developer tool almost.

And, and I think I've, you know, had conversations with our CTOs like, yeah, we should, we should open source this, you know, the same way that like, like, it's almost like a, I don't know how much you look at that stack, but like, like Lankchain has kind of become a little bit of this like agent builder dev tool layer of that stack.
There's like career AI, there's like, I mean, like the foundation models are getting into it as well.
But I think, um,
Yeah, like maybe there's something to make that layer of our stack open source, but I feel like that probably won't be the focus.
Like, you know, the product itself is probably more important.
Yeah.
Like the application layers of it.
Like, for example, we're going to have monitoring tools.
We're going to have ways for you to kind of like measure the performance of your content.
Yeah, so I'll ask Tucker what he was referring to with open source.
mean, maybe he's also referring to just like our whole approach to like doing work.
Like, you know, we always use this term, like we're open sourcing kind of our approach to like what we do for Webflow or Ramp or like, you know, like lovable to do work for them.
As in, we're just kind of like sharing kind of how we build with AI and do with that what you will, you know.
Okay.
Makes sense.
And then I think the other thing I was curious about is you mentioned you have a pretty small cohort and you're looking to scale.
Which makes sense.
And you talked about how the most important thing to you is that value is delivered to this cohort, right?
Like above all else.
How are you measuring engagement today within the cohort?
Like, how are you measuring health?
Yeah, I think the answer is we really don't.
Yeah, I think the answer is we don't.
have some ideas for how we should do it, you know?
But I think we literally started charging for the community like a month and a half ago.
But I think, I mean, there's a lot that I've like looked at as far as, mean, we circle and they like present some metrics for you or it's like, okay, I guess it's how people think about it.
Like we have courses, you know, and there's like kind of course retention at that level.
I think probably at the top level, just as a kind of a signal in general is just like how many people kind of keep paying for it.
We used to do just like a lump sum upfront for the whole year.
But I think specifically for the like, we care a
A bit more about Signal than like optimizing how much revenue we're going to make from the community.
We changed it to monthly.
So I see the cohorts kind of churn every month.
that'll tell me.
Yeah.
really giving them any value.
Yeah.
Circle is a really strong platform.
I've worked with Circle and a lot of other different community platforms.
I've also used community BI tools like Common Room that give you a little bit more insight into the complete user funnel within community.
I'd be really curious with this cohort to see not just who's retaining, but also if there's a correlation to their engagement activity.
For example, like of the people who are continuing every month, are they also people who are attending events, engaging, talking to other people?
Like what does their behavior look like?
Because a lot of people will pay for a service, but not necessarily buy into the community perspective.
They'll consume, but they won't put, they won't contribute to the conversation.
So I'm also curious about that.
I know we're running out of time, so I don't have time to ask you about, like, you said you had some thoughts around what other things you wanted to measure.

Yeah, well, I think, like, exactly what you're bringing up, like, I think the, like, core design or, like, engagement loop or, like, what we're even trying to get people to do, like, hasn't even been designed, to be honest.

I would say most people in the community probably signed up just to get some piece of content that I was gating, you know, like, we do a workshop, and it's like, hey, like, everyone asks, like, even during the workshop, it's like, hey, we have a recording, we have a recording, and it's like, I'm just going to put that in the community, and then people are paying, you know, just to get access to that, and I'm sure, yeah, if you looked at it, they'd probably just, like, take that, maybe they watched, you know, one or two other things, and, like, otherwise, there's almost nothing to even do in the community at the moment.
So, I think designing that is, actually, that's maybe a question for you, is, like, how would you even approach, kind of.
Designing that experience so it doesn't just become like I come to watch a video, right?
Which is like not obviously like that's not a community.
In two minutes, I would say first I'd like to design and think about like what would I like the journey within the community to look like.
Obviously people are opting in to buy to pay for and consume this content, but what is it that I want them to walk away with?
And what do I want them to continue doing in their time in the community?
And then I would design the experience around that, right?
Whether that means after you, let's say, consume content, maybe there's a piece that says put it into practice in the community, right?
Like do X, Y, and Z that's based on the content you actually consumed.
Whether that means creating micro communities within the community itself that address those specific pillars of content or maybe those content activities.
I think that's where I would start.
I can't.
So there's so much that you can do, but I would think about it more from a longer view instead of just like, okay, they're in the community, they're doing this one thing, and then what's next?
Design it, right?
Like, it should feel more like a storyline versus like one activity, one chapter.
That's where I would start.
That makes sense.
I really like the kind of, you got to do something, you know, with this stuff you're learning and investing time and like that needs to somehow live in the community.
I wonder what that is.
Like, I guess, I mean, it's kind of nice because like there's an output to the workshops usually, which is like, hey, you're kind of learning this so you can create a piece of content, you know?
And it's like, I guess we could ask people to kind of post kind of the content they created even and then we'll engage and kind of help them, you know, make it better.
A good example of this, I'll leave you with this in one minute, but a good example of this is Skillshare, right?

Skillshare does this really well, where you'll enroll in Coursera, you'll enroll, you'll complete certain chapters or like parts of it, and then there'll be an actionable part that you share with the community, like, you know, produce this asset, do this work, whatever.
And then people can kind of chime in and give their, you know, their kudos, their feedback.
Honestly, I think that there's so much opportunity here.
It could be such an enriching experience.
And I would love to continue the conversation with the rest of the team, and then just get a better picture of how the community can also serve long term other areas of the business, right?
Like we think about like the funnel itself, but I think there's even more to it.
What I mean by that, like, even at Monarch, when we think about analysis of the themes of the discussions, that feeds into our paid ads.
What are the things that people are actually caring about and talking about, and how do we design a value prop from an ad perspective that actually resonates with future customers?
things like that, I think, is where community really shines in the long term.
It expands even outside of its own value prop.

So, yeah, I could talk about community forever, but if you have, I know our time is up and I want to be respectful of your time, but if you do have any questions or anything like that, I can always send me an email.
I'd be happy to answer.
Cool.
That sounds great.
Yeah, I appreciate your time.
We'll definitely be in touch.
Our process is like pretty straightforward.
I mean, we'll have, I think maybe the one thing is like we didn't spend that much time talking about kind of the things you've done before, so we might do that.
And then we just have this like kind of little assignment take home where we just try to do some like actual work together and then it takes like maybe two hours and then we talk about that.
And then that's.
So, yeah, that's it.
Let me try to move really quickly.
So, yeah, appreciate your time.
I'll definitely sink with Tucker and then, yeah, go from there.
Sounds good.
It was great to meet you.
Enjoy the Oakland fresh air and the amazing food because no amount of sunshine can make up for the lack of great Mexican food and you guys have the best.
There is a food truck called El Ultimo Baile.
It's, I don't know, I mean, they move around your food truck, but if you like Mexican food, highly recommend and I'll be living vicariously for you.
Okay.
I will look at this transcript to get the exact name of that so I can try to find it, but appreciate that.
All right.
Thank you so much and have a good weekend.
You too.
Bye.
Thanks.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening Sheyla Ruiz for the Community Lead role.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=137.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**The Community as a Product:** The "AI-Led Growth" community is a standalone product, not a marketing channel. Its success is measured by member count (goal: 1k by EOY), with retention as the key value signal.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=1988.0)
  - [**Problem: Passive Consumption:** The community is a passive content library. Members join for gated workshop recordings, not for interaction, resulting in low engagement and an undefined user journey.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2409.0)
  - [**Proposed Solution: Active Participation:** Shift from consumption to creation. Design a user journey that requires members to apply learnings (e.g., post content for feedback), creating a virtuous cycle of value and engagement.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2470.0)
  - [**Sheyla's Experience:** Sheyla's work at Monarch Money (financial literacy) and Couchbase (developer tools) shows a track record of building communities with distinct, audience-specific value propositions.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=1864.0)

## Topics

### Problem: Passive Community & Unclear Value

  - [The "AI-Led Growth" community (AIledgrowth.com) has 70 paying members ($150 upfront + $30/mo).](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=490.0)
  - [**Primary Use Case:** Members join to access gated workshop recordings.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2409.0)
  - [**Result:** Low engagement. The community lacks a defined user journey or a clear path for members to apply learnings, making it a passive content library.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2409.0)
  - [**Measurement Gap:** No formal engagement metrics exist. Monthly churn is the only current signal of value.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2288.0)

### Context: GrowthX Agency & Community Origin

  - [**GrowthX Agency:** Builds AI-driven content systems for clients (e.g., Webflow, Ramp), publishing 30–40 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=267.0)
  - [**Community Origin:** Grew from marketing the agency's process. High demand for workshops (500–1k registrants) led to paid access, proving the value of the knowledge itself.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=451.0)
  - [**Differentiator:** The community's value comes from sharing real-world methods from the agency's work, not from pitching a tool.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=770.0)

### Solution: Design for Active Participation

  - [**Core Principle:** A community needs an independent value prop, not just product support.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=1253.0)
      - [**Example (Monarch Money):** The community's value is financial literacy and peer support, not just using the app.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
  - [**Proposed User Journey:**](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2470.0)
      - [**Step 1: Consume Content** → Watch a workshop or course.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2470.0)
      - [**Step 2: Apply Learning** → Complete a task based on the content.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2470.0)
      - [**Step 3: Share Output** → Post the result in the community for feedback.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2556.0)
  - [**Goal:** Shift from passive consumption to active creation and peer interaction.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2470.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Jason Gong:**](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2676.0)
      - [Sync with Tucker on the hiring process.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2676.0)
      - [Schedule a follow-up interview with Sheyla.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2676.0)
      - [Send a 2-hour take-home assignment to evaluate practical skills.](https://fathom.video/share/pdosdh8svwfHeSsYed-Sr9xPaDb5YrU-?tab=summary&timestamp=2676.0)

action_items.description: Email Tucker re: open-source reference; then update Sheyla
Email Sheyla re: next steps: schedule deep-dive w/ team; send take-home; then schedule review
